coredata.dc:title: Flexible semiconductor film based on properties poly(o-phenylenediamine) nano ribbon as biologically active substrate
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Jin-Feng
Sun, Tongming
Zhu, Jinli
coredata.dc:description: Tissue engineering provides a new way for various diseases, the ideal biologically active substrate is the key problem of the tissue engineering. In this paper, poly(o-phenylenediamine) nanoribbon (PoPD NR) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal treatment. The soft hydrophilic PoPD NR papers can be obtained simply via the routine filtration technique. We demonstrated the PoPD NR paper provides an ideal environment for the adhesion, growth and proliferation progress of rat adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells for the first time. The low electrical conductivity, smooth edge and hydrophilic surface of PoPD NR paper which is helpful for cell proliferation. We further demonstrated the superiority of that with low conductive ability for cell proliferation progress. The PoPD NR paper is expected to be a new tissue-engineered graft which can be used in bone, vessel and skin repair.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Conducting polymer
Tissue engineering
Semiconductor
Nano ribbon
Biologically active substrate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Low magnification TEM image of PoPD NR. Inset: digital photograph of PoPD NR ink with a concentration of 1.0mgmL−1. (b) High-resolution TEM image of PoPD NR. (c) FFT of a single PoPD NR. (d) Width and (e) L/W distribution histograms of PoPD NR.

High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C1s and (b) N1s of PoPD NR. (c) Raman spectra of PoPD NR. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of PoPD NR ink. Inset: digital photograph of PoPD NR ink with a concentration of 1.5mgmL−1. (e) The change of absorption strength at 535nm of PoPD NR ink with different concentrations (1.5, 3.5 and 7.5mgmL−1).

(a) Meyer rod coating setup for scalable PoPD NR coating on PET substrate. The PET plastic substrate is put on a flat glass plate and a Meyer rod is pulled over the ink and substrate, which leaves a uniform layer of PoPD NR NW ink. Finished PoPD NR film coating on PET substrate. The PoPD NR coating looks uniform over the entire substrate shown in the figure. (b) Optical transmittance of transparent PoPD NR measured with a UV–vis spectrometer, without including the substrates. (c) Digital photograph of PoPD NR paper which indicates the excellent flexibility of PoPD NR paper. (d) Contact angle of Hy-G paper for water.

Representative fluorescence images showing the cell viability and membrane integrity of rADSCs on PoPD NR paper after (a) 1day and (b) 7 days of incubation, visualized by staining with a LIVE/DEAD® BacLight™ Bacterial Viability Kit (L13152). The live bacteria with intact cell membranes appear green while the dead ones with damaged membranes are red. (c) Cell density cell density after 1, 3, 5 and 7 days of incubation. (d) Cell viability of rADSCs on PoPD NR paper (black), graphene paper (red) and acidulated PoPD NR paper (blue) after 1, 3, 5 and 7 days of incubation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Flexible semiconductor film based on properties poly(o-phenylenediamine) nano ribbon as biologically active substrate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tissue engineering provides a new way for various diseases, the ideal biologically active substrate is the key problem of the tissue engineering. In this paper, poly(o-phenylenediamine) nanoribbon (PoPD NR) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal treatment. The soft hydrophilic PoPD NR papers can be obtained simply via the routine filtration technique. We demonstrated the PoPD NR paper provides an ideal environment for the adhesion, growth and proliferation progress of rat adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells for the first time. The low electrical conductivity, smooth edge and hydrophilic surface of PoPD NR paper which is helpful for cell proliferation. We further demonstrated the superiority of that with low conductive ability for cell proliferation progress. The PoPD NR paper is expected to be a new tissue-engineered graft which can be used in bone, vessel and skin repair.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomaterial is a synthetic material used to replace part of a living system or to function in intimate contact with living tissue [1–4]. A biomaterial is different from a biological material such as bone which is produced by a biological system [5]. Due to the complexity of the physiological environment, in vivo sensing of crucial clinical parameters remains confined to host responses against implantable biosensors such as foreign bodies, especially against foreign biomembranes [6–8]. As a result, implantation of medical devices/biomaterials may trigger tissue injury and in amatory response. To eliminate these problems, a feasible solution is to impart bioactivity and biocompatibility to semiconductor devices for implantation [7,8]. Meanwhile, implantable biomedical devices/materials should also possess good corrosion resistance against the physiological environment for stability and longevity [9,10]. By now, most biomaterial are based on metals (such as: Au, Ag, Pt, Ti and its alloys), polymers (such as: nylon, PTFE, polyester and silicone) and ceramies (such as: carbon, a1uminum oxide and hydroxyapatite) [2–10]. However due to the speed development of tissue engineering, the ideal biologically active substrate is still the key problem of the tissue engineering [6].

Conducting polymers such as: polyaniline, polythiophene, polyparaphenylene, polypyridine and their ramifications are widely used in fluorescent probes [11–15], adsorbents [16–19], electrode materials [20–24] and photocatalysis [25–28]. Previous reports have revealed that the poly(p-phenylenediamine) nanofibers show high-efficiency effects in enhancing the fluorescence intensity of thiols through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [14]. 1H NMR study shows that the strong hydrogen bond between poly(p-phenylenediamine) and thiols is the possible media of FRET progress. The theoretical calculated results further conform to the obtained data [14]. These reports show that the conducting polymers have favorable property and have great potential application in fluorescent switch or logic gate [29]. Liao et al. demonstrated the extensive application prospect of poly-phenylenediamine nano-particles in fluorescence detection [30]. Moreover, Liao et al. demonstrated the low biotoxicity of poly(o-phenylenediamine) which can be used in cell imaging in vitro [31]. All these preliminary studies indicate the semi-conductive conducting polymers are potential ideal materials for tissue engineering [31].

In this work, PoPD NR was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal treatment. The soft hydrophilic PoPD NR papers can be obtained simply via the routine filtration technique. PoPD NR paper provides an ideal environment for the adhesion, growth and proliferation progress of rat adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells (rADSCs). The low electrical conductivity, smooth edge and hydrophilic surface of PoPD NR paper which is helpful for cell proliferation. The PoPD NR paper is expected to be a new tissue-engineered graft which can be used in bone, vessel and skin repair. We further confirmed the affect of electrical conductivity of PoPD NR paper on the cell proliferation progress. The acidulated PoPD NR paper with higher conductivity confirmed the superiority of that with low conductive ability for cell proliferation progress.

All the chemicals (o-phenylenediamine and (NH4)2S2O8) were purchased from Aladdin. (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were made on a Hitachi H-8100 EM (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was performed on a Nicolet 6700 (resolution 0.4cm−1) infrared spectrometer and samples were dispersed in potassium bromide and compressed into pellets. The UV–vis spectra were obtained on a UV5800 Spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out on a PHI Quantera II system (Ulvac-PHI, INC, Japan). MALDI-TOF-MASS spectra were recorded on an ABI Voyager De pro spectrometer.

The typical preparation process of PoPD NR was as follows: 0.4mg oPD and 0.4mg (NH4)2S2O8 was dissolved in 10.0mL water and were under ultrasonication for 1h (200W, 100kHz) to obtain homogeneous dispersion system. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 15mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120°C for a period of 24h, and cooling to room temperature naturally. The yield was about 27%. The solvent of suspension was removed with the aid of a rotary evaporator. And the PoPD NR has excellent solubility in many polar organic solvents or water with different pH levels.

All research was approved by the Ethics Committee and the Animal Research Committee of the Ninth People’s Hospital affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University of Medicine, and informed consent was obtained from each subject. Rat ADSCs (rADSCs) were isolated from the intermuscular fat of 6-week-old male Sprague Dawley rats (Shanghai Animal Experimental Center, China) and cultured in F12/DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS (Invitrogen) and 100 units mL−1 penicillin–streptomycin (Invitrogen), as previously described. For the encapsulation, PoPD NR paper was laid on the bottom of a 24-well plate. Then rADSCs were seeded onto the upper layer of PoPD NR paper at a density of 2×104 cells/well. The 24-well plate was incubated at 37°C for 4h, and then adding 1mL F12/DEME medium into each well slowly. All specimens were incubated at 37°C in an atmosphere with 5% CO2.

A live/dead assay was performed on days 1, 3, 5 and 7. The cell culture medium was removed and the PoPD NR paper was washed with Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline, pH 7.2 (PBS). 1.0mL of medium (without serum) containing 0.001mM calcein-AM and 0.001mM ethidium homodimer-1 (Invitrogen) was added to each specimen. The cells were then observed by a Leica TCS SP8 microscope (Leica Microsystems, Germany). Live cells stained green, dead cells red. Two parameters were measured. First, the percentage of live cells was measured, which was defined as P Live = N Live/(N Live + N Dead), where N Live is the number of live cells and NDead is the number of dead cells in the same image. Three specimens of each material were tested (n =3). Two randomly chosen fields of view were photographed for each specimen for a total of 6 photos per material. The second parameter was living cell morphology. This included rADSCs shape and their distribution.

Fig. 1 a shows low magnification TEM image of PoPD NR which indicates these PoPD NR have uniform width and similar length. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1b) shows that there was no crystalline structure which indicates the amorphous structure of PoPD NR (doubly confirmed using selected electron diffraction pattern in Fig. 1c). The XRD spectrum (Fig. S1) also show PoPD NR have amorphous structure. Further statistical result indicates the PoPD NR are 17±5nm in width and 1–3μm in length (Fig. 1d). The aspect ratio is 160±25 (Fig. 1e).

The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S2) shows two prominent peaks centered at 285 and 399eV correspond to the C 1s and N1s. The N and C ratio is 32.1 at.% [32,33]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s and N 1s were shown in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. The peak centered at 284.5eV is attributed to the CCC linkage [34,35]. The peak centered at 286.2eV is attributed to the CN linkage. The peak centered at 400.6eV is attributed to the NC linkage, the peak centered at 399.1eV is attributed to the CNC linkage and the peak centered at 397.8eV is attributed to the CN linkage [36,37]. The XPS results are doubly confirmed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Fig. S3) analysis. The adsorption peaks at 3325, 3241 and 3213cm−1 corresponding to the NH stretching mode, and implying the presence of secondary amino groups [18]. The strong peaks at 1605 and 1531cm−1 are assigned to CN and CC stretching vibrations in phenazine structure, respectively [14]. The peaks at 1238 and 1371cm−1 are associated with the CN stretching in the benzenoid and quinoid imine units [15]. The peaks at 902 and 831cm−1 can be attributed to the out-of-plane deformation of CH on a 1, 2, 4-tetrasubstituded benzene ring, which implied that the polymers had the basic phenazine skeleton [18]. Moreover, the bands at 755 and 594cm−1, characteristic of CH out-of-plane bending vibrations of benzene nuclei in the phenazine skeleton, are also observed [18,19]. Due to these abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups, the zeta potential of the PoPD NR solution was measured to be ca. −50mV in average, indicating the formation of highly negative charged surface [38]. Raman spectrum of PoPD nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 2c. Obviously, the peak at 1170cm−1 is due to the CH in-plane bending mode, which indicates the presence of quinoid ring, and the weak band at 1200cm−1 is assigned to the CN stretching mode of polaronic units [17–19]. The spectrum also reveals that the peaks located between 1370 and 1420cm−1 are assigned to the CN+ stretching modes of the delocalized polaronic charge carriers [18]. What's more, the peak at 1520cm−1 corresponds to the NH bending deformation mode and the bands at 1549 and 1494cm−1 are ascribed to the CC deformation of benzenoid rings and quinoid rings, respectively [17]. Moreover, the peak at 1622cm−1 is assigned to the benzene ring [19].

Fig. 2d inset shows the PoPD NR aqueous solution (PoPD NR ink) with a concentration of 1.5mgmL−1. The brown ink dispersion is observed to be stable in room temperature for more than 1 month. The stability of the dispersions was further monitored using UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 2c presented the typical UV–vis absorption spectrum of PoPD NR. Obviously, PoPD NR dispersions showed absorption peak at 250 and 341nm, suggesting the maintenance of sp2 carbon structure [39,40]. The absorption peak at 535nm can be attributed to the p–π transition mode. The absorbance at 535nm was chosen as an indicator to reflect the change of PoPD NR content against storing time in the dispersions [37]. The stability of three dispersions with different concentrations (1.5, 3.5 and 7.5mgmL−1) was studied. As shown in Fig. 3 e, no significant loss in absorbance during the initial 144h (6 days) of storing indicates the excellent water dispersibility of PoPD NR. The high dispersibility can be due to the high zeta potential of the PoPD NR [37].

This ink can be used to construct the flexible fluorescent transparent semiconductor film. As shown in Fig. 1d, Meyer rod setup for a lab scale coating of PoPD NR films, which could be easily scaled-up by using slot die or graveur coating. As shown in Fig. 3a, 300μL of PoPD NR ink is dropped on a 200cm2 (10cm×20cm) polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate. Then, a Meyer rod (RDspecialist Inc.) is pulled or rolled over the solution, leaving a uniform, thin layer of PoPD NR ink on the substrate. After the initial drying of the film at 80°C, the coating is further dried at 150°C for 1h. Fig. 3a shows the uniform coating of PoPD NRs on a PET substrate. The PoPD NR film is flexible and transparent. The sheet resistance of obtained film is 60kΩsq−1. The sheet resistance of PoPD NR film can be easily tuned by changing the content of PoPD NR ink. PoPD NR films with different densities on PET substrates fabricated with the simple Meyer rod coating process. When 50, 150, 250, 350 and 450μL of PoPD NR ink is dropped on a 200cm2 (10×20cm) PET substrate, the sheet resistance of obtained film is 154, 102, 67, 56 and 51kΩsq−1, respectively. Fig. 3b shows the optical transmittance over a large wavelength range of PoPD NR films of sheet resistance which is an important property for transparent and conductive electrodes. The transmittance of all these PoPD NR films is higher than 90% from visible light to nearinfrared regions (300–1700nm) which is as well as Ag nanowires. By contrast, the transmittance for the ITO electrode decreases for wavelengths longer than 1100nm [40–42]. More importantly, the constant transmittance from 400 to 1700nm is important when this film is used in solar cells [41,42].

The PoPD NR ink also has advantage in the fabrication of macroscopic materials. To demonstrate this, the PoPD NR aqueous dispersions were used to prepare PoPD NR papers via the routine filtration technique. Typically, 10.0mL of 1.0mgmL−1 PoPD NR aqueous dispersion was filtrated through a 20nm membranes (AAO, Waterman, Germany), followed by drying overnight at 80°C. Interestingly, the filtration of PoPD NR aqueous dispersion could be completed within 240min. A free-standing and bendable PoPD NR paper with a diameter of ca. 4.0cm (Fig. 3c) was obtained after peeling off the membrane. The obtained PoPD NR paper show excellent flexibility (Fig. 3c) and hydrophilic performance. As shown in Fig. 3d, the contact angle of PoPD NR paper for water is 24.2° which implies the potential application value in biotechnological application [43].

The biological properties of obtained PoPD NR paper were further evaluated by rADSCs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, rADSCs encapsulated in PoPD NR paper exhibited excellent viability. Fig. 4a shows cells attached in the PoPD NR paper and have a high cell viability of 90% and the live cell density is 40 cells mm−2 on day 1. The high cell viability indicates the PoPD NR paper have low cytotoxicity which is better than graphene based materials [44–55]. Fig. 4b and c shows a significantly increased cell density (190 cells mm−2) on day 7 that showed the stem cells had a high proliferate potency on the PoPD NR paper. Moreover, the hydrophilic property of PoPD NR paper provides an ideal environment for the adhesion, growth and proliferation progress of rADSCs. As shown in Fig. 4b, rADSCs expended and maintained spindle shape that indicated the PoPD NR paper provided a well environment for the rADSCs adhere. Apparently, rADSCs gathered in a cluster that means cells on the PoPD NR paper having a favorable stem cell pluripotency. Meanwhile, the cell viability of rADSCs on day 3, 5 and 7 is 91%, 93% and 92%, respectively (Fig. 4d).

It deserved to note that, the graphene paper shows much lower cell viability (the cell viability of rADSCs on day 1, 3, 5 and 7 is 91%, 82%, 78% and 72%, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4d) which indicates the advantages of PoPD NR paper. The higher cell viability can be due to the low electrical conductivity (sheet resistance of obtained PoPD NR paper is 20MΩsq−1), smooth edge and hydrophilic surface of PoPD NR paper. To confirm the affect of electrical conductivity of PoPD NR paper on the cell proliferation progress, the acidulated PoPD NR paper was further introduced (preparation process is shown in supporting information). The acidulated PoPD NR paper shows much higher electrical conductivity (sheet resistance of obtained acidulated PoPD NR paper is 340Ωsq−1, meanwhile, the surface pH of acidulated PoPD NR paper is 6.8–7.2 which is similar with original PoPD NR paper) than PoPD NR paper. As shown in Fig. 4d, the cell viability of acidulated PoPD NR paper of rADSCs on day 1, 3, 5 and 7 is 73%, 62%, 58% and 52%, respectively which is much lower than original PoPD NR paper. Thus, all above results indicate the PoPD NR paper can be used as a biologically active substrate with good biocompatibility and cytocompatibility. The PoPD NR paper is expected to be a new tissue-engineered graft which can be used in bone, vessel and skin repair.

In summary, PoPD NR was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal treatment. The soft hydrophilic PoPD NR papers can be obtained simply via the routine filtration technique. The low electrical conductivity, smooth edge and hydrophilic surface of PoPD NR paper which is helpful for cell proliferation. The PoPD NR paper is expected to be a new tissue-engineered graft which can be used in bone, vessel and skin repair. We further confirmed the affect of electrical conductivity of PoPD NR paper on the cell proliferation progress. The acidulated PoPD NR paper with higher conductivity confirmed the superiority of that with low conductive ability for cell proliferation progress. This work is helpful to design and fabrication of new biologically active substrates.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476117), the Science and Technology Projects Fund of Nantong City (BK2014014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, athttp://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2015.12.027.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Orthop. Res. Off. Publ. Orthop. Res. Soc.
